name: Ace of Diamonds & Herkimer Diamonds Mine
short_description: A fee dig site in Herkimer County specializing in Herkimer diamonds - double-terminated quartz crystals found nowhere else in the world with the same clarity and quality.
description:
Ace of Diamonds & Herkimer Diamonds Mine is a working fee dig operation in the heart of Herkimer County, New York, home to the world-famous Herkimer diamonds. Despite their name, these are not diamonds but rather exceptional double-terminated quartz crystals that form in dolostone host rock unique to this region. The site sits in gently sloping terrain at 604 feet elevation, making it accessible for families and beginners while still offering rewarding collecting opportunities.

Herkimer diamonds are among the most sought-after quartz specimens in the world, prized for their brilliant clarity, perfect termination, and distinctive shape. Unlike mined diamonds, these crystals form through natural processes in cavities within 500-million-year-old Ordovician dolostone. The mine allows visitors to dig through the host rock to extract these crystals themselves, providing a hands-on experience of real rockhounding.

The fee dig model means you pay for access and keep what you find, making this an excellent choice for beginners who want professional guidance and a concentrated collecting area. The site typically provides tools and instruction, and staff can help identify specimens and explain the unique geology. Best results come from systematically working the exposed rock faces and carefully breaking apart matrix material.

Collecting season runs year-round, though spring through fall offers the most pleasant weather and highest success rates. Winter conditions can be icy and muddy, making digging more difficult. The gentle slope and established paths make this suitable for families with children ages 8 and up, and most visitors can spend several hours here finding multiple specimens.
history: Herkimer diamonds form in Ordovician-age dolostone (approximately 450-480 million years old) that was deposited in a shallow tropical sea. Silica-rich solutions percolated through cavities in the rock, crystallizing into these unique double-terminated quartz crystals with exceptional clarity. The Herkimer County mining district has been actively worked since the early 1800s, with commercial mining and collecting sites continuing to operate today, making this region one of America's most productive quartz crystal localities.
tips: Focus your digging on natural fractures and weathered areas in the dolostone matrix where crystals are more likely to be exposed and loosened. Use a small hammer and chisel to carefully work away matrix rock without striking the crystals directly - Herkimer diamonds are brittle and can break if hit with force. Look for the distinctive double-pointed terminations protruding from the rock; color ranges from clear to smoky, and larger specimens (over 1 inch) are prized. Bring water to rinse specimens as you find them, which helps reveal clarity and quality.
terrain_notes: The site occupies gently sloping terrain at 604 feet elevation with established dig areas and pathways. The ground is a mix of exposed dolostone bedrock and broken matrix material. Walking surfaces are relatively even but uneven in spots where digging has occurred. Most collecting areas are within easy walking distance of parking, typically less than 100 feet to the main dig zones. Footing is stable in dry conditions but can become muddy or slippery after rain.
best_season: April - October. Spring through fall offers the most pleasant weather for outdoor digging, with April-May and September-October providing ideal temperatures and lower crowds. Summer heat can be intense during midday, so early morning and late afternoon are preferable. Winter is possible but muddy and cold conditions make digging uncomfortable and less productive.
written_directions: From the Town of Fairfield (approximately 6 miles south), head north on State Route 29 or local roads toward Herkimer. The mine is located in the Herkimer diamond district near Little Falls. GPS coordinates 43.13146, -74.974136 will direct you to the site. Look for signage marking the mine entrance. Parking is typically available on-site with a small unpaved lot.
road_conditions: Paved roads to the site. Local roads to the mine entrance may be unpaved but are passable by standard vehicles.
parking_notes: On-site unpaved parking area with capacity for multiple vehicles. Well-marked mine entrance.
collection_rules: Personal use collection only at this fee dig site. You pay the daily dig fee and keep all specimens you find within the permitted dig areas. No commercial reselling of specimens without prior written permission from the operator. Digging is restricted to designated areas only. All tools must be provided by or approved by the mine operator.
quantity_limits: Keep what you find within the day-use period purchased.
rules:
Personal use only
Keep what you find
Dig only in designated areas
Use only approved tools or those provided
No commercial collection without permission
Respect other collectors and safety rules
nearest_services: Town of Fairfield (6 miles south) has limited services. Little Falls (8 miles north) offers restaurants, gas stations, and accommodations. Herkimer village (4 miles north) has additional services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Unpaved parking area and established walking paths to dig zones. No wheelchair access to active dig areas due to uneven ground and need to climb over and around matrix rock. Sturdy footwear recommended for walking on broken rock. Minimal elevation change makes it suitable for those with moderate fitness levels.

faq.question: What exactly are Herkimer diamonds and where do you find them at this mine?
faq.answer: Herkimer diamonds are double-terminated quartz crystals found exclusively in Herkimer County, New York. Despite their name, they are not true diamonds but exceptional quartz specimens prized for their clarity and perfect termination. At Ace of Diamonds, you dig through 450-million-year-old dolostone matrix to extract these crystals from natural cavities in the rock. Specimens typically range from small points to larger 1-2 inch crystals.

faq.question: How much does it cost to dig for Herkimer diamonds at this fee site?
faq.answer: Ace of Diamonds & Herkimer Diamonds Mine operates as a fee dig site where you pay a daily access fee and keep all specimens you find. Specific fee amounts vary by season and dig area, so contact the mine directly for current pricing. The fee-dig model is beginner-friendly because it provides access to a concentrated, productive area with professional guidance without worrying about weight limits or collection restrictions.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect Herkimer diamonds at Ace of Diamonds?
faq.answer: No permit is required for this private fee dig site. You only need to pay the daily dig fee to access the mine and collect specimens. The mine operator handles all land permissions, and your fee covers access to designated dig areas and use of the site facilities. This makes it an excellent choice for visitors who want to rockhound without navigating permit requirements.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to dig for Herkimer diamonds near Fairfield?
faq.answer: April through October offers the best conditions, with spring (April-May) and fall (September-October) providing ideal temperatures and lower crowds. Summer heat can be intense, so early morning or late afternoon visits are preferable. Winter is possible but muddy, cold conditions make digging uncomfortable and less productive. Most collectors recommend visiting during shoulder seasons for comfort and success.

faq.question: What tools do I need to dig for Herkimer diamonds at this location?
faq.answer: The mine typically provides or approves specific tools for safe digging. A small hammer and cold chisel are essential for carefully working away dolostone matrix without damaging the brittle quartz crystals. A brush or small whisk broom helps clear dust from specimens as you dig. Bring water and a soft cloth to rinse crystals for inspection. Gloves and safety glasses are recommended for protecting your hands and eyes from sharp rock fragments.

faq.question: Is Ace of Diamonds mine suitable for families and children rockhounding near Herkimer County?
faq.answer: Yes, this fee dig site is very family-friendly and suitable for children ages 8 and up. The gentle slope, easy access, and concentrated collecting area mean kids can find specimens quickly and feel successful. The site provides instruction and guidance, making it perfect for beginners. Professional staff can help identify finds and explain the geology. Bring sunscreen, water, and snacks for a full day of digging with the whole family.

meta_title: Herkimer Diamonds at Ace of Diamonds Mine, NY
meta_description: Dig for Herkimer diamonds and quartz crystals at Ace of Diamonds Mine in Herkimer County, NY. Family-friendly fee dig with specimens to keep.
